title: EU Unlocks $106 Billion Loan for Ukraine — Here's What Took So Long
summary:
Have you ever been in a situation where you desperately needed money, but someone kept blocking you from getting it? That's exactly what Ukraine has been going through — except the amount at stake is $106 billion, and the person blocking it was the prime minister of Hungary.

This week, the European Union finally approved a 90 billion-euro ($106 billion) loan for Ukraine, and it couldn't have come at a more critical moment. The International Monetary Fund estimates Ukraine faces a financing gap of roughly 136 billion euros ($158 billion) over the next two years. Without this EU loan — which covers about two-thirds of that gap — Ukrainian officials warned they could have run out of resources to sustain basic state functions and the war effort as early as this spring.

So why did it take so long? For months, outgoing Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán — widely seen as the Kremlin's closest ally inside the 27-nation EU — blocked the deal. The holdup was tied to a dispute over the Druzhba oil pipeline, which carries Russian oil to Slovakia and Hungary. The pipeline went offline in late January after Ukrainian officials said it was damaged in a Russian attack. Hungary and Slovakia accused Ukraine of deliberately cutting off supplies, turning the issue into a political standoff.

The breakthrough came after Orbán lost an election earlier this month. Then, this week, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced that repairs on the pipeline were complete and oil would resume flowing to Slovakia and Hungary. That removed the final obstacle, and on Thursday, EU leaders unanimously approved changes to the EU's long-term budget to allow for the spending.

Here's how the money will be used: roughly a third will go toward budgetary support for Ukraine's government — things like paying salaries, keeping hospitals open, and maintaining basic services. The remaining two-thirds will be directed to defense, covering weapons procurement and expanding domestic arms production.

And here's the most unusual part: Ukraine doesn't have to start repaying the loan until Russia pays war reparations. Instead of using Russia's frozen central bank assets to guarantee the loan — which could trigger Russian retaliation or legal challenges — EU member states decided to borrow the money themselves and lend it to Ukraine. The first tranche of 45 billion euros ($53 billion) is expected to be released in the coming months, with another 45 billion euros available in 2027.

For Ukraine, this loan isn't just about money — it's about survival. And for the EU, it's a powerful statement of solidarity with a country fighting for its existence.
why_it_matters: This loan shows how European solidarity can be tested by internal politics, and it raises important questions about how the West will fund Ukraine's long-term survival and reconstruction without relying on Russia's frozen assets.

keywords.explanation: A political and economic union of 27 European countries that make decisions together
The country that Russia invaded in 2022 and that now needs financial support
The prime minister of Hungary who blocked the loan until he lost an election
A major pipeline that carries Russian oil to countries in Europe, including Hungary and Slovakia
Payments that a country that caused a war must make to the country it harmed
The process of buying weapons and military equipment for the armed forces

questions.question: Why did Hungary's prime minister block the EU loan for months?
options: He wanted Ukraine to fix an oil pipeline first
He wanted the money to go to Hungary
questions.correct_answer: He wanted Ukraine to fix an oil pipeline first

questions.question: How much of Ukraine's financing gap does the EU loan cover?
What part of the loan will be used for buying weapons?
When will Ukraine have to start repaying the loan?
options: In 2027 when the second tranche is released

questions.question: What caused the Druzhba oil pipeline to go offline in January?
options: A Russian attack that damaged it
questions.correct_answer: A Russian attack that damaged it

questions.question: How did EU leaders finally approve the loan this week?
options: By unanimously changing the EU's long-term budget
questions.correct_answer: By unanimously changing the EU's long-term budget

background_read: Since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, the EU has provided Ukraine with over €100 billion in financial, military, and humanitarian aid, making it Ukraine's largest supporter.

The EU's decision-making requires unanimity among all 27 member states for many budget and foreign policy matters, meaning a single country can block action—this is why Viktor Orbán's opposition was so powerful.

The Druzhba pipeline, built in the 1960s, is one of the world's longest oil pipelines, supplying refineries in several European countries. Its shutdown in January 2025 threatened energy supplies for Hungary and Slovakia, which rely heavily on Russian oil.

The idea of using frozen Russian central bank assets (about $300 billion) to fund Ukraine's recovery has been debated in the West, but legal and political risks made the EU choose to borrow the money itself instead.
Article_Structure: LEAD: EU finally approves a $106 billion loan for Ukraine after months of delays.
└─ specific: 'It couldn't have come at a more critical moment' – Ukraine faced running out of money by spring.
KEY EVENT: The loan covers two-thirds of Ukraine's $158 billion financing gap.
├─ WHO: EU leaders approved it unanimously; Viktor Orbán blocked it until he lost an election.
├─ WHEN: Approved this week after the pipeline dispute ended.
└─ AMOUNT: 90 billion euros ($106 billion), split into two tranches (2025 and 2027).
├─ ONE SIDE: Ukraine desperately needed the funds for survival and war effort.
└─ OTHER SIDE: Hungary used the Druzhba pipeline issue as leverage to block the loan.
EVIDENCE: IMF estimates of Ukraine's needs; pipeline damages; Zelenskyy announces repairs.
OPEN QUESTION: Can Russia be forced to pay war reparations to trigger loan repayment?

perspectives.description: The loan provides critical resources for Ukraine to keep its government running and to produce more weapons domestically, which could change the course of the war.

perspectives.perspective: Negative – Delay caused by one country
perspectives.description: Hungary's months-long blockade of needed aid shows how the EU's unanimity rule can be exploited by a pro-Russian leader, leaving Ukraine hanging in a life-or-death situation.
Instead of using Russia's frozen assets as collateral, the EU itself is borrowing the money to lend to Ukraine, a novel approach that avoids legal retaliation but adds debt to EU members.
The loan's unusual repayment condition tied to Russian war reparations may never be fulfilled, which could lead to the EU eventually having to forgive the debt or find another solution.
